Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - BEYOND OBJECT RECOGNITION: A NEW BENCH MARK TOWARDS OBJECT CONCEPT LEARNING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Understanding objects is a central building block of artificial intelligence, especially for embodied AI. Even though object recognition excels with deep learning, current machines still struggle to learn higher-level knowledge, e.g., what attributes does an object have, what can we do with an object. In this work, we propose a challenging **Object Concept Learning** (OCL) task to push the envelope of object understanding. It requires machines to reason out object affordances and simultaneously give the reason: _what attributes make an object possesses these affordances_ . To support OCL, we build a _densely_ annotated knowledge base including extensive labels for three levels of object concept: categories, attributes, and affordances, and their causal relations. By analyzing the causal structure of OCL, we present a baseline, Object Concept Reasoning Network (OCRN). It leverages causal intervention and concept instantiation to infer the three levels following their causal relations. In experiments, OCRN effectively infers the object knowledge while follows the causalities well. Data and code will be publicly available.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Object understanding is essential for intelligent machines. Recently, benefited by deep learning and large-scale datasets (Deng et al., 2009; Lin et al., 2014), category recognition (Krizhevsky et al., 2012; Ren et al., 2015) has made tremendous leaps of progress. But to close the gap between human and machine perception, machines need to pursue deeper object understanding, e.g., recognizing higher-level attributes (Isola et al., 2015) and affordances (Gibson, 2014), which may help them establish the concept of objects (Martin, 2007) when interacting with complex contexts.

Category apple is a symbol indicating its referent (real apple). In line with symbol grounding (Harnad, 1990), machines should learn knowledge beyond category to approach concept understanding. According to cognition studies (Ross, 2008; Martin, 2007), attributes depicting objects from the physical/visual side play an important role in object understanding. Thus, many works (Lampert et al., 2009; Xiao et al., 2010; Farhadi et al., 2009) studied to ground objects with attributes, e.g., a hammer consists of a long handle and a heavy head. Moreover, attributes can depict object _state_ (Isola et al., 2015). An elegant characteristic of attributes is cross-category: objects of the same category can have various states (big or fresh apple), whilst various objects can have the same state (sliced orange or carrot). If the category is the **first** level of object concept, the attribute can be seen as the **second** level closer to the physical fact.

However, recognizing attributes is still far away from concept understanding. Given a hammer, we need to know it can be held to hit nails. That is, requiring machines to infer affordance (Gibson, 2014) indicating what actions humans can perform upon objects. Here, we refer to affordance as the **third** level, which is closely related to common sense and causal inference (Gibson, 2014). Although affordance learning has been studied in robotics (Do et al., 2018; Hermans et al., 2011) and vision (Chuang et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2014) communities in decades, it is still challenging. First, previous works (Nguyen et al., 2017; Fouhey et al., 2015) often focus on recognizing affordance solely. But we usually infer affordance based on attribute observation. When we need to knock in a nail without a hammer at hand, we may find other hard or heavy objects instead, e.g., a thick book. This profoundly reveals the **causality** between attribute and affordance. Second, previous works are designed for category/scale/scene-limited tasks, e.g., in Zhu et al. (2014), 40 objects and 14 affordances are included; Hermans et al. (2011) collect 375 indoor images of 6 objects, 21

To reshape object learning, we believe it is essential to look at the above three levels in a **unified** and **causal** way based on an extensive knowledge base. Hence, we move a step forward to propose the object concept learning (OCL) task: given an object, machines need to infer its category, attributes, and further answer “ _what can we do upon it and why_ ”. In a nutshell, machines need to reason affordance based on object appearance, category, and attributes. To this end, we build a largescale and dense dataset consisting of **381** object categories, **114** attributes, and **170** affordances. It contains **80,463** images of diverse scenes, **185,941** object instances in different states. Different from previous works (Chao et al., 2015; Hermans et al., 2011; Zhu et al., 2014), OCL offers a more nuanced angle. It includes: (1) **category** -level attribute ( _A_ ) and affordance ( _B_ ) labels; (2) **instance** - level attribute ( _α_ ) and affordance ( _β_ ) labels. Besides, we consider and annotate the _causal relations_ between three levels to evaluate the reasoning ability of models and keep the follow-up methods from fitting data only. Accordingly, based on the causal structure of OCL, we also propose a _neurocausal_ method, **O** bject **C** oncept **R** easoning **N** etwork ( **OCRN** ), as the future baseline. It operates the _instantiation_ from category-level to instance-level, and leverages _causal intervention_ (Pearl et al., 2016) to alleviate the bias from categories and infer attributes and affordances. OCRN outperforms a host of baselines and shows impressive performance while follows the causal relations well.

In summary, our contributions are threefold: (1) Introducing the object concept learning (OCL) task poses challenges and opportunities for deeper object understanding and knowledge-based reasoning. (2) Building a dataset consists of diverse objects, elaborate attributes and affordances, together with their causal relations to benchmark OCL. (3) An object concept reasoning network (OCRN) is introduced to reason three levels with concept instantiation and debiasing, which performs well.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Object Attribute.** Attribute depicts the visual/physical properties like color, size, shape, etc. It usually plays the role of intermedia between pixels and higher-level concepts, e.g., prompting object recognition (Farhadi et al., 2009), affordance learning (Hermans et al., 2011), zero-shot learning (Lampert et al., 2009), and object detection (Kumar Singh et al., 2018). Recently, several largescale datasets (Farhadi et al., 2009; Xiao et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2017; Patterson & Hays, 2016; Isola et al., 2015; Krishna et al., 2016; Hudson & Manning, 2019) are released. For attribute recognition, recent works have made progress. Beside direct multi-label classification (Lampert et al., 2009; Parikh & Grauman, 2011; Xiao et al., 2010; Patterson & Hays, 2016) or leveraging the correlation between attribute-attribute and attribute-object (Hwang et al., 2011; Chen & Grauman, 2014; Mahajan et al., 2011), intrinsic properties (compositionality, contextuality (Misra et al., 2017; Nagarajan & Grauman, 2018), symmetry (Li et al., 2020b)) of attribute-object are also proven useful.

**Object Affordance.** The concept of affordance is introduced by Gibson (2014). Affordance learning has two canonical paradigms: direct mapping (Fouhey et al., 2015) or indirect method (Zhu et al., 2014; Zhao & Zhu, 2013; Wang et al., 2017; Roy & Todorovic, 2016) with intermedia like object category, attribute, and 3D contents. Some studies also tried to learn affordance from human-object interactions, i.e., encoding the relationship between object and action (Gupta & Davis, 2007; Yao et al., 2013; Kato et al., 2018). Visual Genome (Krishna et al., 2016) provides visual relationships between objects in the scene graph, including some human actions instead of affordances. However, these relationships cover limited and sparse affordances. Differently, we use easily accessible object images as the knowledge source, and _densely_ annotate all attributes/affordances for all object instances. Besides the vision community, the robot community also pays much attention to affordance (Do et al., 2018; Pinto & Gupta, 2016; Thermos et al., 2017; Pinto et al., 2016), especially for

(2014) built a knowledge base containing attribute, affordance, human pose, and human-object spatial configuration. But labeling pose and human-object relative location are costly. Chao et al. (2015) proposed a _semantic_ category-level affordance dataset including 91 COCO objects (Lin et al., 2014) and 957 affordances.

**Causal Inference.** There is increasing literature on exploiting causal inference (Pearl et al., 2016) in machine learning, especially with causal graphical models (Spirtes et al., 2000; Pearl et al., 2016), including feature selection (Guyon et al., 2007) and learning (Chalupka et al., 2014), video analysis (Pickup et al., 2014; Lebeda et al., 2015), reinforcement learning (Nair et al., 2019; Dasgupta et al., 2019), etc. Recently, Wang et al. (2020a) studied the causal relation between objects in images and used intervention (Pearl et al., 2016) to alleviate the observation bias in detection. Atzmon et al. (2020) analyze the causal generative model of compositional zero-shot learning and disentangle the representations of attributes and objects. In this work, we explore the causal relations between three object levels and apply backdoor adjustment (Pearl et al., 2016) to alleviate the existing bias.

## 3 KNOWLEDGE BASE CONSTRUCTION

Our goal is to construct a dataset that can characterize abundant knowledge in object category, attribute, affordance, and causal relations. The construction process is given as follows (Fig. 2).

**Data Collection. (1) Affordance** : We collect 170 affordances out of 1,006 candidates from widelyused action/affordance datasets (Chao et al., 2015; Gu et al., 2018; Chao et al., 2018; Gupta & Malik, 2015; Zhu et al., 2014; Nguyen et al., 2017) in view of generality and commonness. **(2) Category** : Considering the taxonomy (WordNet (Fellbaum, 2012)) and diversity, we collect 381 objects out of 1,742 candidates from object datasets (Farhadi et al., 2009; Xiao et al., 2010; Patterson & Hays, 2016; Liu et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2014). **(3) Attribute** : When viewed through the lens of the causal relation between attribute-affordance, we manually filter 500 most frequent attributes from large-scale attribute datasets (Farhadi et al., 2009; Xiao et al., 2010; Patterson & Hays, 2016; Liu et al., 2017; Krishna et al., 2016) and choose 114 attributes, covering colors, deformations, supercategories, various surface, geometrical, and physical properties. **(4) Image** : We extract 75,578 images from object datasets (Farhadi et al., 2009; Xiao et al., 2010; Patterson & Hays, 2016; Liu et al., 2017; Krishna et al., 2016), together with their Ground Truth (GT) boxes. To ensure diversity, we further manually collected 4,885 Internet images of selected categories. Then, we annotate the missing box and category labels for all instances via crowdsourcing. Finally, **185,941** instances of **381** categories from **80,463** images are collected, i.e., average 488 instances per category and 2.31 boxes per image. More details and class lists are in _Appendix Sec. A.11_ . OCL is long-tail distributed, where the head categories have over 5,000 instances each, but the rarest categories have only 9 instances, which challenges the robustness of vision systems.

**Attribute Annotation.** We offer annotations in two levels: (1) **Category-level attribute** ( _A_ ) contains common sense. For each _category_ , we annotate its **most common** attributes. In concept learning, the usage of category-level annotation as common knowledge can date back to Osherson et al. (1991). Following Osherson et al. (1991), to avoid bias, annotators are given _category-attribute_

(2) **Instance-level attribute** ( _α_ ) is the individual attributes of **each instance** . The annotation unit is an _attribute-instance pair_ . Each pair is labeled by multi-annotator.

**Affordance Annotation.** There are also two levels of granularity: (1) **Category-level affordance** _B_ , similar to _A_ , is annotated in _category-affordance pairs_ , indicating the common affordances of each category. Following Chao et al. (2015), the annotators label category-level affordance matrix _MB_ ([381 _,_ 170]) similar to _A_ annotation. (2) **Instance-level affordance** _β_ is annotated for **every instance** with the help of _object states_ (Isola et al., 2015). As _B_ is determined by common states, objects in specific states may have different affordances from _B_ , e.g., we cannot board a flying plane. Moreover, if a service robot finds a broken cup, it may infer that the cup can still hold water as it is trained with _B_ labels. Thus, we need detailed _β_ beyond _B_ . As the instances in the same state should have similar _β_ (all rotten apples cannot be eaten), six experts first conclude the states. In total, **1,376** states are defined, and each category has 3.6 states on average. Next, _β_ is annotated for each state and the instances are assigned with _beta_ according to their states. More details please refer to _Appendix Sec. A.2_ It is worth noting that, as states seem to be a beneficial latent variable for OCL, in practice, we do not use it in models because the states we use are a _subset_ of whole state space, and there can be _unseen_ states in real-world data.

**Causal Relation Annotation.** We annotate _instance_ -level (considering the context of each instance) causality to answer _which attribute(s) are the critical and direct causes of a certain affordance?_ **(1) Filtering** to avoid the combinatorial explosion. Initially, we need to make binary decisions on all _instance-α-β_ triplets, which is far beyond handleable. Fortunately, we find that **most** _α_ - _β_ classes (e.g., shiny and kick) are meaningless and always of no causality on any instances. Thus, we can firstly exclude the most impossible pairs, meanwhile, guarantee the completeness of causality. Finally, we obtain about 10% _α_ - _β_ classes as candidates. **(2) Instance-level causality** : we also adopt object states as a reference. Multiple annotators have been involved for each _state-α-β_ triplet and are asked whether the specific attribute is the _direct_ cause of this affordance in this state. The answers are combined and checked for all instances of a state. Finally, we obtain about 2 M _instance-α-β_ triplets of causal relations. As we have labelled all _α_ and _β_ for all instances, the causal relations would be in four situations: [0,0], [1,1]; [0,1], [1,0]. The former two are “positive”, e.g., fresh(1/0) _→_ eat(1/0) for an apple. While the last two are “negative”, e.g., broken(1/0) _→_ drive(0/1) for a _car_ .

**Discussion.** Fig. 3 shows some examples of OCL. These clear causalities are not thoroughly studied in previous datasets (Zhu et al., 2014; Nguyen et al., 2017; Fouhey et al., 2015; Hermans et al., 2011). We compare OCL with previous dense datasets in Tab. 1. More analysis figures and tables are in _Appendix Sec. A.3_ .

## 4 OBJECT CONCEPT LEARNING

## 4.1 TASK OVERVIEW
Given an instance _I_ (image region in box _bo_ ), OCL aims to infer attribute _α_ and affordance _β_ while following the causalities. Formally, OCL can be described as:

_< Pα, Pβ >_ = _F_ ( _I, P_ ( _O|I_ )) _,_ ∆ _Pβ_ = _TDE_ [ _F_ ( _I, P_ ( _O|I_ ))] _,_ (1)

where _Pα, Pβ_ are the probabilities of _α, β_ , given an object instance _I_ and its predicted category probability _P_ ( _O|I_ ). ∆ _Pβ_ means the Total Direct Effect ( _TDE_ [ _·_ ]) (Pearl et al., 2016) of affordance prediction change after we operate upon a model _F_ ( _·_ ). ∆ _Pβ_ is expected to follow the GT causal relation between attribute-affordance. We will detail the reasoning evaluation in Sec. 5.

We spilt images into train, validation (val) and test sets with 56,916:14,446:9,101 images. The val and test sets cover 221 of the 381 categories, and the train set covers all categories. OCL is a long-tailed recognition task and requires generalization to cover the whole object category-attributeaffordance space with imbalanced information. Thus, it is challenging for current vision systems without the reasoning ability to understand the causalities.

## 4.2 OBJECT CONCEPT REASONING NETWORK

Here, we first analyze the causal structure of OCL and then propose a basic system, Object Concept Reasoning Network (OCRN), as the future baseline for the OCL task.

## 4.2.1 ATTRIBUTE ESTIMATION

To implement Eq. 3, we use _mean_ object category feature to represent category-level attribute _A_ . We take the mean feature ( _f_[¯] _Oi_ ) of all instances in one category as the representation of the category ( _Oi_ ). It is then mapped to attribute feature space: _fAi_ = _FA_ ( _f_[¯] _Oi_ ), where _FA_ ( _·_ ) is a fully-connected layer (FC) (Fig. 5). The representation set of _A_ is _fA_ = _{fAi}[m] i_ =1[.][For each category, we use] _[ f][I]_[of] its all instances (train set) to calculate _f_[¯] _Oi_ in training and inference.

**Attribute Instantiation.** Next, we obtain _α_ representation according to Eq. 3:

where _POi_ is the _prior_ category probability ( _P_ ( _Oi_ ) in Eq. 3). Eq. 4 indicates the attribute _instantiation_ from _A_ to _α_ with _I_ as the _condition_ . Hence, we can equally translate the _α_ estimation problem into a **conditioned instantiation problem** . _Fα_ ( _·_ ) is implemented with multi-head attention mechanism (Vaswani et al., 2017), as shown in Fig. 5. Both _fAi_ and _fI_ are fed to three independent linear transformations to obtain key, query, and value vectors _K, Q, V_ and _K[′] , Q[′] , V[′]_ . **Discussion** . Overall, OCL poses extreme challenges to current AI systems. It expects representative learning to accurately recognize attribute and affordance from raw data meanwhile causal inference to capture the causalities within diverse instances and contexts, i.e., both the _intuitive System 1 and logical System 2_ (Bengio, 2019). From the experiments, we find that models struggle to achieve satisfying results on all tracks simultaneously. Notably, it is difficult to achieve a satisfying TDE score via data fitting, and there is much room for improvement. For future studies, a harmonious performance on _α, β_ , and causality learning are encouraged to capture object knowledge better. Potential directions may include _causal representation learning_ (Sch¨olkopf et al., 2021), _casual reinforcement learning_ (Bareinboim, 2020), _neural-symbolic reasoning_ (Besold et al., 2017), etc.

## 6 CONCLUSION

In this work, we introduce object concept learning (OCL), which expects machines to infer affordances and explain what attributes enable an object to possess these affordances. Accordingly, we build an extensive real-world dataset and present a baseline OCRN based on casual intervention and instantiation. OCRN achieves decent performance and follows the causalities well. However, OCL remains challenging and would inspire a line of studies upon reasoning-based object understanding.

## 7 ETHICS STATEMENT

This work primarily proposes a daily object dataset to advance machine understanding of objects for the future study of embodied AI, intelligent robots, etc. Our work may help the home service robots better understand the human living scenes and then help to improve our lives, especially for the health care of patients, elders, and children. All the data used in OCL are publicly available. We have cited the creators and followed the official licenses. For all images, we only provide the URL links in our future web for research uses only. Moreover, modern deep learning models are usually computationally expensive. Our proposed model OCRN also needs multiple GPUs to train. In the future, OCL encourages a new line of studies to understand objects’ common sense and combine causal reasoning and deep learning. We believe the future works focusing on learning the causality instead of heavily fitting data would be more computationally lite, less data-hungry, and low-carbon.

## 8 REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENT

We provide our source code, OCL dataset samples, and a video demo in the supplementary material (the zip file). It includes all the manuscripts to preprocess and load data, construct the OCRN model with the hyper-parameters, train and test the model, evaluate the performance on the OCL benchmark, etc. Please refer to the code file for more details. The details of related datasets, OCL data processing and annotation are in the main text Sec. 3, Appendix Sec. A.3, A.11. Our code and data will be publicly available.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper introduces an ambitious and well-motivated task (Object Concept Learning) with a substantial new dataset and a causal reasoning framework. The attempt to unify categorical, physical (attribute), and functional (affordance) levels of object understanding is a significant contribution to embodied AI. However, there are technical concerns regarding the notation used in the causal derivations, specifically inconsistencies in the variable indices and the description of the deconfounding process. Additionally, the 'instantiation' mechanism and the specific implementation of the TDE score calculation are slightly underspecified, which may hinder exact reproducibility despite the provided code. The causal graph and the use of 'Total Direct Effect' are conceptually sound but require more rigorous formal alignment with Pearl's structural causal models to fully justify the quantitative claims in Section 5.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and timely contribution by introducing the Object Concept Learning (OCL) task, a densely annotated dataset with causal structure, and a neurocausal baseline model (OCRN) grounded in causal intervention. The motivation is strong, the dataset construction is thorough, and the use of causal reasoning via backdoor adjustment is both principled and well-motivated. The experimental validation is extensive, including ablation studies and multiple evaluation metrics. However, there are several technical concerns, particularly regarding the derivation and consistency of the causal intervention formulation in the model, where notation and expectations are not fully aligned with standard causal definitions. There is also ambiguity in the computation of TDE and how it relates to model outputs. While the core ideas are sound and the work is impactful, some critical derivations need clarification to ensure correctness and reproducibility.

comments.explanation: The paper defines the debiased attribute representation as the expectation of _Fα_ ( _fI , fAi_ ) with respect to _P(Oi)_, which corresponds to the backdoor adjustment. However, the text states that _P(Aj|Oi) = 1_ if _i = j_ and 0 otherwise, which is correct given the deterministic mapping from category to its category-level attribute. But then it proceeds to use _P(Oi)_ as the prior for the expectation in Eq. 3 and its implementation in Eq. 4. This would only be correct if the adjustment is over _O_, which is a confounder. However, in standard backdoor adjustment, one conditions on the confounder, not marginalizes over it with its prior. The correct form should be a conditional expectation given _O_, not a prior-weighted sum. This suggests a confusion between marginalization and conditioning in the causal adjustment. If the model computes an expectation over all category features weighted by prior frequency, it does not deconfound but instead reinforces population-level bias. The intention—applying backdoor adjustment—is sound, but the implementation as written would not eliminate the confounding effect of category.

comments.explanation: The paper claims to use the _do_-intervention to eliminate the edge from _O_ to _I_, but this does not align with standard causal semantics. _O_ (category) is a label derived from _I_ (image appearance), so logically _I_ should be upstream of _O_, not the other way around. The causal graph (Fig. 4) shows _O_ as a parent of _A_ and _B_, but not of _I_, which makes sense if _O_ is abstract. However, stating that the intervention is on _I_ to eliminate the effect of _O_ is backwards—_O_ is determined by _I_, not the cause of it. The confounder in attribute prediction is _O_ because of spurious correlation, so the correct adjustment should be conditioning on (or blocking) _O_, not applying _do(I)_. The notation _P(α|do(I))_ is thus misleading, as intervening on _I_ would alter the input, not fix a confounding path. The intended use of backdoor adjustment through _O_ is valid, but the framing via _do(I)_ misrepresents the causal logic.

comments.explanation: The paper defines TDE as the total direct effect, but then operationalizes it as the change in _P(βq)_ when masking the feature of _αp_. However, true TDE in Pearl's framework requires holding other variables constant and intervening on _αp_, not just masking its feature. The use of zero-masking approximates feature ablation, but it's not equivalent to a do-intervention unless the model's architecture ensures that the ablated feature has no indirect influence. Moreover, the text says TDE is computed for all _[αp, βq]_, but the scores depend on ground truth labels _GTβq_, which introduces label leakage into the causal evaluation. The scoring in Eq. (8) uses _GTβq_ to determine the sign, which conflates accuracy with causal effect size. A proper TDE measure should assess the causal magnitude and direction independently of label correctness. This risks rewarding models that align with dataset frequencies rather than true causal mechanisms.

comments.title: Unclear implementation of affordance instantiation with _fBi_
comments.quote: Given the conditions _{fI , fα[′][, f][B] i[}]_[,] _[ F][β]_[(] _[·]_[)][ operate the instantiation.] _[F][B]_[(] _[·]_[)] _[,][ F][β]_[(] _[·]_[)][ are implemented] the same as _FA_ ( _·_ ) _, Fα_ ( _·_ ), except the first input condition of _Fβ_ is the concatenation of _fα[′][, f][I]_.
comments.explanation: The description states that _Fβ(·)_ is implemented similarly to _Fα(·)_ using attention, but with inputs _[fα′, fI]_ concatenated. However, the attention mechanism previously used in _Fα_ takes _fI_ and _fAi_ as inputs to generate keys, queries, and values. It's unclear how the category-level affordance feature _fBi_ is integrated into _Fβ(·)_, since the text says _fBi_ is used but does not specify its role in the attention. Is it used as a condition, a query, or part of the key/value? The omission makes it impossible to reproduce the affordance instantiation module. Given that this is central to the model, the underspecification is significant.

comments.title: Inconsistency in reported OCRN performance across tables
comments.explanation: In Table 2, OCRN reports _α_: 31.6, _β_: 53.3, TDE: 9.5, _α-β_-TDE: 9.2. However, in Table 3, the same model reports _α_: 32.4, _β_: 52.2, TDE: 20.5, _α-β_-TDE: 17.0. The two tables supposedly evaluate the same OCRN model (with _LT DE_), yet the scores differ significantly, especially in TDE (+11 points). The text does not explain whether these are test vs. val, different runs, or different model variants. Since Table 3 is labeled as ablation on the val set, it's likely Table 2 is test set. But without explicit clarification, this discrepancy could mislead readers about model performance. While not an error per se, the lack of labeling reduces transparency.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper introduces an interesting new task (OCL) and dataset for object concept learning that goes beyond simple recognition to include attributes, affordances, and their causal relations. The dataset construction appears thorough and the proposed OCRN model shows some improvements over baselines. However, there are several technical issues in the causal inference formulation and mathematical notation that need clarification. The causal intervention implementation is not fully justified, and some key equations appear problematic or inconsistent with standard causal inference conventions.

comments.title: Inconsistent backdoor adjustment notation
comments.quote: P(α|do(I)) = Σ_{i=1}^m P(α|I, A_i) P(O_i)
comments.explanation: The backdoor adjustment formula in Equation (3) uses P(O_i) (prior category probability) rather than P(O_i|I) as would be standard in backdoor adjustment. The authors state that P(A_j|O_i) = 1 if i=j and 0 otherwise, but then don't properly account for this in the summation. Standard backdoor adjustment would typically condition on the actual confounder value given the observation, not use the prior distribution.

comments.title: Incorrect TDE loss formulation
comments.quote: L_TDE = Σ_{p,q} max(0, τ - |P(β_q) - P_TDE[α_p](β_q)|)
comments.explanation: The hinge loss in Equation (7) appears incorrect for the stated goal of maximizing differences for causal pairs. If τ=0.1, this loss would actually minimize the difference (penalize when |P(β_q) - P_TDE[α_p](β_q)| > 0.1). To maximize differences, the formulation should encourage larger values, not smaller ones. The current implementation contradicts the stated objective.

comments.title: Undefined notation in TDE score
comments.quote: TDE^{α_p}_{β_q} = |P(β_q) - P_TDE[α_p](β_q)| × P(α_p = GT_α_p) × P(β_q = GT_β_q)
comments.explanation: In Equation (8), the notation P(α_p = GT_α_p) and P(β_q = GT_β_q) is unclear. These appear to be indicator functions (1 if correct, 0 otherwise) rather than probabilities, but this isn't specified. If they are indeed indicators, the notation is misleading. This formulation also biases the TDE score toward easily predicted attributes/affordances rather than purely measuring causal understanding.

comments.title: Ambiguous causal graph description
comments.quote: As I indicates the physical noumenon, it is the source of semantic and functional properties and decides α, β
comments.explanation: The claim that appearance I 'decides' attributes and affordances is philosophically and technically problematic. In a causal framework, I would typically be an effect of underlying properties, not their cause. The causal graph in Fig. 4 shows I→α and I→β edges, but this directionality needs better justification in the context of object properties.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for causal intervention implementation
comments.quote: we assign zero-mask to the feature of α_p and keep the other attribute features, to get P_TDE[α_p](β_q)
comments.explanation: The paper doesn't adequately justify why zero-masking attribute features constitutes a proper causal intervention. In causal inference, do-calculus implies changing the causal mechanism, not just masking features. The authors need to explain why this feature-level approximation is valid and how it relates to true causal intervention in the underlying generative process.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency for priors
comments.quote: f_α = Σ_{i=1}^m F_α(f_I, f_Ai) P_Oi
comments.explanation: The notation P_Oi is used in Equation (4) without being clearly defined. From context, it appears to represent P(O_i), but this should be explicitly stated. The paper sometimes uses P(O_i) and sometimes P_Oi, creating inconsistency. Also, the expectation over priors in Eq. 4 doesn't match the conditional formulation in Eq. 3.

comments.title: Questionable claim about TDE performance
comments.quote: OCRN shows greater improvements and outperforms Attention with 2.5 and 1.4 mAP on two TDE tracks
comments.explanation: The absolute TDE scores (9.5 and 9.2 mAP in Table 2) are quite low, suggesting the models are not truly learning causal relationships well. The claim of 'greater improvements' should be tempered by acknowledging that overall TDE performance remains poor, indicating this remains a significant challenge.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: This paper introduces a new task (Object Concept Learning) and an accompanying large-scale dataset, framed through the lens of causal inference. The proposal to move beyond simple object recognition toward understanding causal relationships between object categories, attributes, and affordances is compelling and timely for embodied AI. The methodology, utilizing causal intervention and backdoor adjustment, is well-motivated. However, the paper suffers from significant notation inconsistencies and mathematical ambiguity in the formal definitions of its core reasoning mechanism, specifically regarding the handling of conditional probabilities and indices in the proposed network architecture.

comments.title: Mathematical ambiguity in causal intervention formalization
comments.quote: where $m = 381$. $A_j$ is the category-attribute vector of $j^{th}$ category. As $A$ is decided by $O$, $P(A_j|O_i) = 1$ iif $i = j$ and $P(A_j|O_i) = 0$ if $i = j$, where $O_i$ is the $i^{th}$ category and $A_j$ is the category-attribute of $j^{th}$ category.
comments.explanation: The condition $P(A_j|O_i) = 0$ if $i = j$ is a likely typo for $i \neq j$. Furthermore, the logic mapping the category index to the attribute vector seems to conflate the random variable $A$ (a vector of attributes) with a discrete index $j$. If $A_j$ is a vector, $P(A_j | O_i)$ is undefined in standard probability notation unless $A_j$ is treated as a specific event representing the attribute configuration of category $j$. The index consistency is confusing and renders the derivation of the intervention formula difficult to verify.

comments.title: Notation collision in attribute instantiation equation
comments.quote: $f_{\alpha} = \sum_{i=1}^m P(O_i) F_{\alpha}(f_I, f_{Ai})$
comments.explanation: The notation uses $f_{\alpha}$ to denote the debiased feature representation, but Eq. (4) (implicitly referenced here) and later sections represent $f_{\alpha}$ as an 'instantiated representation' being passed through a classifier. In Section 4.2.1, the authors use $f_{\alpha}$ to refer to both the output of the expectation (the global debiased feature) and the feature map before the softmax in the attention mechanism. This overloading makes it ambiguous whether the downstream classifiers $g_{\alpha}$ are operating on the expected 'debiased' feature or the individual instantiated features.

comments.title: Undefined indexing and summation in affordance estimation
comments.quote: For causal inference operation (Sec. 5), $f_{\alpha}$ is first separated to attribute categorywise features $f_{\alpha p}$ by multiple independent FCs, where $f_{\alpha p}$ is the feature of $p^{th}$ attribute. The features are aggregated via concatenating-compressing by an FC to $f_{\alpha}'$.
comments.explanation: The transition from the debiased representation $f_{\alpha}$ (which is an expectation over categories) to the individual attribute-specific features $f_{\alpha p}$ is mathematically underspecified. There is no clear description of how a single feature vector $f_{\alpha}$ can be meaningfully decomposed into $p$ distinct attribute-specific features without an inverse operation or latent factor disentanglement that is not detailed. It is unclear if $f_{\alpha p}$ are derived from $f_I$ conditioned on the category-level $f_{\alpha}$ or if they are extracted directly from the aggregated $f_{\alpha}'$.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a challenging benchmark and a baseline model for object concept learning, with dense annotations of attributes, affordances, and causal relations. The dataset construction is thorough, and the problem formulation is compelling. However, the core methodological contribution—causal intervention for debiasing—suffers from a critical inconsistency between the described causal graph and the intervention performed. The graph (Fig. 4) does not include an edge from object category O to image appearance I, yet the intervention is explicitly used to 'eliminate the edge from O to I.' Without this edge, the justification for the backdoor adjustment formula is unsupported, undermining the theoretical basis of the OCRN model. Additionally, the derivation of the intervention formula is not fully justified given the graph structure. These issues significantly weaken the paper's central technical claim.

comments.title: Causal graph missing the edge that intervention targets
comments.quote:
We use the causal graph to shed light on the subtle causalities of OCL in Fig. 4. Causal graph (Pearl et al., 2016) indicates the underlying causalities between variables O, A, B; I, α, β.

To tackle this problem, we propose OCRN utilizing intervention (Pearl et al., 2016) to deconfound the confounder O for α (Fig. 5). That said, in α estimation, we use do(·) operation (Pearl et al., 2016) to eliminate the edge from O to I, i.e., P(α|do(I)) is ...
comments.explanation: The text describes a causal graph in Fig. 4 that should contain an edge from object category O to image appearance I, because the intervention is meant to remove that edge. However, the figure (described in the text as having an inner triangle for O, A, B and an outer triangle for I, α, β) does not include any directed edge from O to I. Without this edge, O is not a confounder for the I-α relationship in the given graph, and the backdoor adjustment formula P(α|do(I)) = Σ_i P(O_i) P(α|I, O_i) lacks the structural justification needed for do-calculus. This is a major inconsistency because the entire debiasing mechanism of OCRN relies on this intervention. The paper does not clarify whether an implicit instantiation edge is intended or if the graph is incomplete.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for the intervention formula
comments.quote: P(α|do(I)) = Σ_j P(α|I, A_j) Σ_i P(A_j|O_i) P(O_i)
comments.explanation: The derivation of the intervention formula from the causal graph is not clearly explained. The graph includes variables O, A, B, I, α, β, but the formula introduces A_j (category-level attribute vector) and O_i (object category) in a double summation. The paper states that P(A_j|O_i) = 1 if i=j and 0 otherwise, which effectively reduces to a single sum, but the graph structure that would license this adjustment is not spelled out. Crucially, the confounder O is not shown to have a direct causal path to I in the graph, so the standard backdoor criterion (which requires all backdoor paths from I to α that go through O be blocked by conditioning on O) does not apply in the way assumed. The transition from the graph to Eq. 3 needs more rigorous justification or a clearer statement of assumed causal structure.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a novel Object Concept Learning (OCL) task and dataset, along with a baseline method (OCRN) that attempts to address category bias via causal intervention. The motivation is clear, and the dataset construction is extensive. However, the manuscript suffers from significant mathematical and theoretical gaps. Key equations implementing the proposed causal intervention are missing or garbled, and there is a fundamental misalignment between the authors' use of `do`-calculus notation and the actual backdoor adjustment/marginalization technique described. Additionally, performance claims in the ablation study contradict the reported numerical results, and the evaluation/training metrics for causal reasoning lack precise formulations. Addressing these issues is essential for verifying the causal claims and ensuring reproducibility.

comments.title: Misapplication of Do-Calculus Notation vs. Backdoor Adjustment
comments.quote: That said, in α estimation, we use do(·) operation to eliminate the edge from O to I, i.e., P(α|do(I)) is [Eq. 2 missing] where m = 381. Aj is the category-attribute vector... P(Aj|Oi) = 1 if i=j... P(Oi) is the prior probability
comments.explanation: The authors aim to remove category bias (confounding) in attribute prediction. They invoke `do(I)` to eliminate the edge `O -> I`, claiming this deconfounds `O`. However, in Pearl's framework, intervening on `I` (cutting incoming arrows to `I`) makes `I` independent of `O`, but it does *not* block the backdoor path `O -> A -> α` (or `O -> B -> β`). The actual implementation described marginalizes over categories using priors `P(Oi)` and category-level attributes `A`, which corresponds to backdoor adjustment `∑_i P(α|A_i, I)P(O_i)` or similar. Calling this `P(α|do(I))` is theoretically incorrect and conflates input intervention with confounding adjustment. This mislabeling undermines the causal inference claims and needs a rigorous reformulation to match standard deconfounding practices.

comments.title: Missing Equations for Causal Intervention and TDE Score/Loss
comments.quote:
i.e., P(α|do(I)) is
[Eq. 3 follows immediately with text description]
We set TDE score TDE_βq^αp as:
[Formula missing]
formulated as:
[Formula missing]
comments.explanation: Critical mathematical formulations are absent from the text (likely exacerbated by OCR extraction, but the gaps remain in the provided manuscript). Specifically, Eqs. 2, 3, 5, 6, and 7 that operationalize the intervention and instantiation are missing or entirely garbled. Furthermore, Sec. 5 states 'We set TDE score... as:' and 'formulated as:' without providing the explicit formulas for the TDE evaluation metric and the hinge loss `L_TDE`. Since the paper's core contribution hinges on causal reasoning and debiasing, the absence of these equations prevents readers from verifying the derivations or reproducing the training objective. The authors must restore these formulations.

comments.title: Discrepancy Between Textual Claim and Ablation Results for Deconfounding
comments.quote: Both α and β performances significantly decline, as explained by Fig. 7 and 8.
comments.explanation: In the Ablation Study (Sec. 5), the authors state that removing deconfounding causes significant performance declines. However, Table 3 shows that 'w/o deconfounding' yields α=32.1 and β=51.8, compared to the full model's α=32.4 and β=52.2. This is a minimal drop of 0.3 mAP, which does not align with the word 'significantly'. While Figs. 7 and 8 demonstrate that deconfounding reduces model bias on the training set, the aggregate metric improvement is marginal. The authors should clarify whether the benefit of deconfounding primarily lies in robustness or generalization to rare/long-tail categories rather than overall mAP, or adjust the textual claim to match the numerical evidence.

comments.title: Underspecified Affordance Instantiation Module
comments.quote: F_β(·) are implemented the same as F_A(·), F_α(·), except the first input condition of F_β is the concatenation of f_α', f_I.
comments.explanation: The attribute instantiation `F_α` is detailed with a specific attention mechanism (queries, keys, values derived from `f_I` and `f_Ai`, softmax scaling, and linear compression). For the affordance module `F_β`, the description is too vague. It is unclear how the concatenated features `cat(f_α', f_I)` are projected into the Q/K/V spaces, whether the same multi-head cross-attention structure is used, or if the feature dimensions are aligned before concatenation. Given that attention mechanisms are sensitive to input sequence lengths and dimensionality, this lack of detail leaves a key architectural choice implicit, hindering faithful reproduction.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper introduces a compelling Object Concept Learning (OCL) task and a large-scale, richly annotated dataset to benchmark causal reasoning in object understanding. The proposed OCRN method attempts to address category bias through intervention, and the experimental setup, including the TDE metric for causal evaluation, is well-conceived. However, the paper contains critical ambiguities in the causal notation used to define the intervention (using $do(I)$ instead of $do(O)$), an omitted derivation for the intervention step, and a minor typo in the reference list. While the core ideas are strong, these issues hinder the precise mathematical interpretation of the debiasing mechanism.
